analogy: Think of "Structure of Atom" through the chapter's own opening images: The rich diversity of chemical behaviour of different elements can be traced to the differences in the internal structure of atoms of these elements. Unit 2 strUctUre of atom After studying this unit you will be able to • know about the discovery of electron,... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Structure of Atom is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that the rich diversity of chemical behaviour of different elements can be traced to the differences in the internal structure of atoms of these elements.

The text explains that unit 2 strUctUre of atom After studying this unit you will be able to • know about the discovery of electron, proton and neutron and their characteristics; • describe Thomson, Rutherford and Bohr atomic models; • understand the important features of the quantum mechanical model of atom; • u n d e r s t a n d n a t u r e o f electromagnetic radiation and Planck’s quantum theory; • explain the photoelectric effect and describe features of atomic spectra; • state the de Broglie relation and Heisenberg uncertainty principle; • define an atomic orbital in terms of quantum numbers; • state aufbau principle, Pauli exclusion principle and Hund’s rule of maximum multiplicity; and • write the electronic configurations of atoms.

The text explains that the existence of atoms has been proposed since the time of early Indian and Greek philosophers (400 B.C.) who were of the view that atoms are the fundamental building blocks of matter.

The text explains that according to them, the continued subdivisions of matter would ultimately yield atoms which would not be further divisible.

The text explains that the word ‘atom’ has been derived from the Greek word ‘a-tomio’ which means ‘uncut-able’ or ‘non-divisible’.

The text explains that these earlier ideas were mere speculations and there was no way to test them experimentally.

The text explains that these ideas remained dormant for a very long time and were revived again by scientists in the nineteenth century.

The text explains that the atomic theory of matter was first proposed on a firm scientific basis by John Dalton, a British school teacher in 1808.

The text explains that his theory, called Dalton’s atomic theory, regarded the atom as the ultimate particle of matter (Unit 1).

The text explains that dalton’s atomic theory was able to explain the law of conservation of mass, law of constant composition and law of multiple proportion very successfully.

The text explains that however, it failed to explain the results of many experiments, for example, it was known that substances like glass or ebonite when rubbed with silk or fur get electrically charged.

The text explains that in this unit we start with the experimental observations made by scientists towards the end of nineteenth and beginning of twentieth century.

The text explains that these established that atoms are made of sub-atomic particles, i.e., electrons, protons and neutrons — a concept very different from that of Dalton. objectives

The text explains that 30 CHEMISTRY 2.1 Discovery of sUb-atomic Particles An insight into the structure of atom was obtained from the experiments on electrical discharge through gases.

The text explains that before we discuss these results we need to keep in mind a basic rule regarding the behaviour of charged particles : “Like charges repel each other and unlike charges attract each other”.

The text explains that 2.1.1 Discovery of electron In 1830, Michael Faraday showed that if electricity is passed through a solution of an electrolyte, chemical reactions occurred at the electrodes, which resulted in the liberation and deposition of matter at the electrodes.

The text explains that the electrical discharge through the gases could be observed only at very low pressures and at very high voltages.

The text explains that the pressure of different gases could be adjusted by evacuation of the glass tubes.

The text explains that these are not deflected by the electric and magnetic fields and have a very high penetrating power through the matter and that is the reason that these rays are used to study the interior of the objects.

The text explains that these rays are of very short wavelengths (∼0.1 nm) and possess electro-magnetic character (Section 2.3.1).

The text explains that a stream of high energy α–particles from a radioactive source was directed at a thin foil (thickness ∼ 100 nm) of gold metal.

The text explains that according to Thomson model of atom, the mass of each gold atom in the foil should have been spread evenly over the entire atom, and α–particles had enough energy to pass directly through such a uniform distribution of mass.

The text explains that it was expected that the particles would slow down and change directions only by a small angles as they passed through the foil.

The text explains that however, when a body is moving in an orbit, it undergoes acceleration even if it is moving with a constant speed in an orbit because of changing direction.

The text explains that these were: (i) Dual character of the electromagnetic radiation which means that radiations possess both wave like and particle like properties, and (ii) Experimental results regarding atomic spectra.

The text explains that 2.3.1 Wave nature of electromagnetic radiation In the mid-nineteenth century, physicists actively studied absorption and emission of radiation by heated objects.

The text explains that it is now a well-known fact that thermal radiations consist of electromagnetic waves of various frequencies or wavelengths.

The text explains that first active study of thermal radiation laws occured in the 1850’s and the theory of electromagnetic waves and the emission of such waves by accelerating charged particles was developed in the early 1870’s by James Clerk Maxwell, which was experimentally confirmed later by Heinrich Hertz.

The text explains that these fields are transmitted in the forms of waves called electromagnetic waves or electromagnetic radiation.

The text explains that it was only in the 19th century when wave nature of light was established.

The text explains that maxwell was again the first to reveal that light waves are associated with oscillating electric and magnetic character (Fig.

The text explains that although electromagnetic wave motion is complex in nature, we will consider here only a few simple properties.

The text explains that (i) The oscillating electric and magnetic fields produced by oscillating charged Fig.2.6 The electric and magnetic field components of an electromagnetic wave.

The text explains that these components have the same wavelength, frequency, speed and amplitude, but they vibrate in two mutually perpendicular planes.

The text explains that 38 CHEMISTRY particles are perpendicular to each other and both are perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave.

The text explains that simplified picture of electromagnetic wave is shown in Fig.

The text explains that (ii) Unlike sound waves or waves produced in water, electromagnetic waves do not require medium and can move in vacuum.

The text explains that (iii) It is now well established that there are many types of electromagnetic radiations, which differ from one another in wavelength (or frequency).

The text explains that some examples are: radio frequency region around 106 Hz, used for broadcasting; microwave region around 1010 Hz used for radar; infrared region around 1013 Hz used for heating; ultraviolet region around 10 16 Hz a component of sun’s radiation.

The text explains that these radiations are characterised by the properties, namely, frequency (ν ) and wavelength (λ).

The text explains that it is defined as the number of waves that pass a given point in one second.

The text explains that wavelength should have the units of length and as you know that the SI units of length is meter (m).

The text explains that since electromagnetic radiation consists of different kinds of waves of much smaller wavelengths, smaller units are used.

The text explains that 2.7 shows various types of electro-magnetic radiations which differ from one another in wavelengths and frequencies.

The text explains that in vaccum all types of electromagnetic radiations, regardless of wavelength, travel at the same speed, i.e., 3.0 × 108 m s–1 (2.997925 × 10 8 ms–1 , to be precise).

The text explains that the frequency (ν ), wavelength (λ) and velocity of light (c) are related by the equation (2.5). c = ν λ (2.5) Fig.

The text explains that 39 STRUCTURE OF ATOM The other commonly used quantity specially in spectroscopy, is the wavenumber ( ).

The text explains that it is defined as the number of wavelengths per unit length.

The text explains that its units are reciprocal of wavelength unit, i.e., m–1 .

The text explains that calculate the wavelength of the electromagnetic radiation emitted by transmitter.

The text explains that which part of the electromagnetic spectrum does it belong to? solution The wavelength, λ, is equal to c/ν, where c is the speed of electromagnetic radiation in vacuum and ν is the frequency.

The text explains that substituting the given values, we have   c v This is a characteristic radiowave wavelength.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 2: Structure of Atom

prelims_traps: Do not treat 30 CHEMISTRY and 2.1 Discovery of sUb-atomic as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Structure of Atom; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Structure of Atom through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 30 CHEMISTRY and 2.1 Discovery of sUb-atomic to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
